(粟生 隆寛, *Aō Takahiro*; born 6 April 1984) is a Japanese former [professional boxer](/wiki/Professional_boxer "Professional boxer") who competed from 2003 to 2018. He is a [world champion in two weight classes](/wiki/Weight_class_(boxing) "Weight class (boxing)"), having held the [World Boxing Council](/wiki/World_Boxing_Council "World Boxing Council") (WBC) [featherweight](/wiki/Featherweight "Featherweight") title in 2009 and the WBC [super-featherweight](/wiki/Super-featherweight "Super-featherweight") title from 2010 to 2012.

WORLD BOXING editorial department (3 October 2003). [矢代、粟生の2ホープがバレラのキャンプに参加](http://www.boxing.jp/news03/news_d/d_0310.htm) (in Japanese). MACC Publications Inc. Retrieved 23 February 2011. (the fourth article from the bottom)

["Barrera encontró sparring idóneo"](http://www.oem.com.mx/esto/notas/n380402.htm) (in Spanish). [ESTO](/wiki/Organizacion_Editorial_Mexicana "Organizacion Editorial Mexicana"). 14 August 2007. Retrieved 23 February 2011.
["Barrera sees a different outcome"](http://www.mb.com.ph/node/30349). *[Manila Bulletin](/wiki/Manila_Bulletin "Manila Bulletin")*. 29 August 2007. Retrieved 23 February 2011.
Jason Pribila (17 March 2009). ["Óscar Larios: A Boxing Tragedy Waiting to Happen"](http://www.secondsout.com/columns?ccs=793&cs=70418). SecondsOut.com. Retrieved 18 March 2009.
12 March 2009 – 14 July 2009 | Succeeded by [Elio Rojas](/wiki/Elio_Rojas "Elio Rojas") |
26 November 2010 – 27 October 2012 | Succeeded by [Gamaliel Díaz](/wiki/Gamaliel_D%C3%ADaz "Gamaliel Díaz") |

**Takahiro Ao** (粟生 隆寛, *Aō Takahiro*; born 6 April 1984) is a Japanese former [professional boxer](/wiki/Professional_boxer "Professional boxer") who competed from 2003 to 2018. He is a [world champion in two weight classes](/wiki/Weight_class_(boxing) "Weight class (boxing)"), having held the [World Boxing Council](/wiki/World_Boxing_Council "World Boxing Council") (WBC) [featherweight](/wiki/Featherweight "Featherweight") title in 2009 and the WBC [super-featherweight](/wiki/Super-featherweight "Super-featherweight") title from 2010 to 2012.

## Professional career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Takahiro_Ao&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Professional career")]

Ao turned professional in September 2003 at the [Korakuen Hall](/wiki/Korakuen_Hall "Korakuen Hall"), Tokyo. In his debut Ao defeated fellow Japanese boxer Hiroshi Kashihara with a second round [knockout](/wiki/Knockout "Knockout").

Ao is managed by the [Teiken Boxing Gym](/wiki/Teiken_Boxing_Gym "Teiken Boxing Gym") in Tokyo, and trains under [Sendai Tanaka](/wiki/Sendai_Tanaka "Sendai Tanaka")'s guidance. He has often attended [Marco Antonio Barrera](/wiki/Marco_Antonio_Barrera "Marco Antonio Barrera")'s training camp since 2003,[[1]](#cite_note-1) because he was appreciated for his speed and heavy fists,[[2]](#cite_note-2) and served as Barrera's chief sparring partner in August 2007.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

After thirteen unbeaten fights, Ao beat Koji Umetsu for the Japanese featherweight title in March 2007.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Ao lost via split decision to champion [Óscar Larios](/wiki/%C3%93scar_Larios "Óscar Larios") on 16 October 2008. Despite flooring Larios in the 4th round, Ao managed to lose a decision in the close and contested bout.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Fortunate for him, Ao got another chance at Larios' title on 12 March 2009. There, he applied more offensive tactics which resulted Larios dropping in the 12th round. Ao won the match by unanimous decision.[[4]](#cite_note-4) On his first defense of the title held on July 14 that same year, Ao lost to [Elio Rojas](/wiki/Elio_Rojas "Elio Rojas") by unanimous decision.
